probe_designs.content: Compute AUROC, AUPRC, sensitivity, specificity, and Brier score separately for each of the 6 ethnicity subgroups. Compare the gap between the best- and worst-performing groups and test statistical significance via bootstrap confidence intervals. A healthy signal: subgroup metrics overlap within bootstrap CIs and disparities are <5% absolute. Unhealthy: large gaps (>10% AUROC) between majority (e.g., White) and minority groups (e.g., Hispanic, Asian, Native American), indicating the model under-serves underrepresented populations.
possible_sources: Chen et al., 'Why Is My Classifier Discriminatory?' NeurIPS 2018
Seyyed-Kalantari et al., 'CheXclusion: Fairness gaps in deep chest X-ray classifiers', PSB 2021
Röösli et al., 'Bias at warp speed: how AI may contribute to disparities gap', JAMIA 2022
MIMIC-III benchmark (Harutyunyan et al., 2019)

probe_designs.probe_name: Equalized Odds and Demographic Parity Audit
probe_designs.content: Evaluate fairness criteria including Equal Opportunity (TPR parity), Equalized Odds (TPR + FPR parity), Demographic Parity, and Predictive Parity (PPV) across ethnicity subgroups at a clinically relevant operating threshold. Compute the maximum absolute difference and disparate impact ratio (target >0.8 per the four-fifths rule). Healthy: TPR/FPR differences <0.05 across groups. Unhealthy: large TPR gaps suggest minority patients are systematically under-flagged for mortality risk.
possible_sources: Hardt et al., 'Equality of Opportunity in Supervised Learning', NeurIPS 2016
Fairlearn toolkit (Microsoft)
AIF360 (IBM)
Pfohl et al., 'An empirical characterization of fair machine learning for clinical risk prediction', JBI 2021

probe_designs.content: Plot reliability diagrams and compute Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Maximum Calibration Error (MCE), and Hosmer-Lemeshow statistics overall and stratified by ethnicity. Probabilistic mortality outputs must be well-calibrated for clinical use. Healthy: ECE <0.05 across all subgroups, slope ~1, intercept ~0 in Cox calibration. Unhealthy: systematic over/under-prediction in particular subgroups (e.g., overestimating mortality for one ethnicity), which would mislead triage decisions.
possible_sources: Guo et al., 'On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks', ICML 2017
Pleiss et al., 'On Fairness and Calibration', NeurIPS 2017
Van Calster et al., 'Calibration: the Achilles heel of predictive analytics', BMC Medicine 2019

probe_designs.probe_name: Class Imbalance and Subgroup Representation Audit
probe_designs.content: Inspect distribution of (a) mortality outcome (positive class typically ~10-13% in MIMIC), (b) ethnicity counts (typically heavily White-skewed in MIMIC-III), and (c) joint outcome×ethnicity cells. Compute representation ratios and check for cells with <30 positive examples (statistically unstable). Healthy: each subgroup has sufficient positive cases and predictions reflect base rates. Unhealthy: extreme imbalance, with rare subgroups effectively unevaluable, causing inflated overall metrics that mask poor minority performance.
possible_sources: Johnson et al., 'MIMIC-III, a freely accessible critical care database', Scientific Data 2016
Ghassemi et al., 'A Review of Challenges and Opportunities in ML for Health', AMIA CRI 2020
Larrazabal et al., 'Gender imbalance in medical imaging datasets', PNAS 2020

probe_designs.content: Track training and validation loss/AUROC across epochs. Compare final train vs. held-out test AUROC. Examine if validation curve plateaus or diverges. Healthy: small generalization gap (<3% AUROC), validation loss monotonically decreasing then plateauing. Unhealthy: large train-test gap suggesting overfitting to TF-IDF noise (10k sparse features can easily memorize), or no learning signal at all if loss flatlines near baseline.
possible_sources: Goodfellow et al., 'Deep Learning' textbook chapter on regularization
Harutyunyan et al., 'Multitask learning and benchmarking with clinical time series data', Scientific Data 2019
Belkin et al., 'Reconciling modern machine learning practice and the bias-variance trade-off', PNAS 2019

probe_designs.probe_name: Input Perturbation and Feature Ablation Robustness
probe_designs.content: Perturb the inputs in controlled ways: (a) zero-out the ethnicity one-hot vector to test whether the model relies on demographics rather than clinical content, (b) randomly drop top-k TF-IDF tokens, (c) inject synthetic noise into TF-IDF vectors. Measure prediction change (KL divergence, AUROC degradation). Healthy: predictions degrade gracefully and don't pivot solely on ethnicity. Unhealthy: removing ethnicity drastically changes predictions (suggesting demographic shortcut learning) or small TF-IDF perturbations cause large output swings (brittle).
possible_sources: Geirhos et al., 'Shortcut Learning in Deep Neural Networks', Nature Machine Intelligence 2020
Ribeiro et al., 'Beyond Accuracy: Behavioral Testing of NLP Models with CheckList', ACL 2020
Adebayo et al., 'Sanity Checks for Saliency Maps', NeurIPS 2018

probe_designs.probe_name: Top-Token Importance and Subgroup Feature Attribution
probe_designs.content: Use logistic-regression-style coefficient inspection or SHAP/LIME on TF-IDF features to identify the top-N tokens driving mortality predictions overall and per ethnic subgroup. Check whether clinically meaningful terms (e.g., 'pressors', 'intubation', 'sepsis') dominate, vs. spurious correlations (e.g., hospital ward names, ethnicity-correlated language). Healthy: clinically interpretable, stable across subgroups. Unhealthy: subgroup-specific reliance on non-clinical artifacts indicates leakage or bias.
possible_sources: Lundberg & Lee, 'A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions', NeurIPS 2017
Caruana et al., 'Intelligible Models for HealthCare', KDD 2015
Jin et al., 'Is BERT Really Robust?', AAAI 2020

probe_designs.probe_name: Temporal and Outcome Leakage Detection in Notes
probe_designs.content: Verify that clinical notes used as input were authored before mortality outcome determination. Search for terms like 'expired', 'deceased', 'comfort care', 'withdrawal of care', 'morgue' that directly reveal the label. Quantify how much AUROC drops when these terms are removed from the TF-IDF vocabulary. Healthy: minimal AUROC drop after sanitization, indicating the model learns prognostic signals. Unhealthy: dramatic drop indicates the model exploits outcome leakage rather than predicting risk.
possible_sources: Boag et al., 'What's in a Note? Unpacking Predictive Value in Clinical Note Representations', AMIA CRI 2018
Lehman et al., 'Do We Still Need Clinical Language Models?', CHIL 2023
Kaufman et al., 'Leakage in Data Mining: Formulation, Detection, Avoidance', KDD 2011

probe_designs.content: For each test patient, hold the TF-IDF features fixed and re-run inference while swapping the one-hot ethnicity vector to each of the other 5 categories. Measure prediction shift distribution (mean absolute difference, max swing). Healthy: small, clinically negligible shifts (<0.02 in probability) since clinical content should dominate. Unhealthy: large shifts indicate the model encodes ethnicity as a strong direct predictor — a fairness red flag often called 'individual unfairness' or counterfactual unfairness.
possible_sources: Kusner et al., 'Counterfactual Fairness', NeurIPS 2017
Garg et al., 'Counterfactual Fairness in Text Classification through Robustness', AIES 2019
Prabhakaran et al., 'Perturbation Sensitivity Analysis to Detect Unintended Model Biases', EMNLP 2019

probe_designs.probe_name: Decision Curve Analysis and Clinical Net Benefit
probe_designs.content: Beyond AUROC, perform Decision Curve Analysis (DCA) across a range of threshold probabilities to estimate clinical net benefit vs. 'treat-all' and 'treat-none' baselines, stratified by ethnicity. Also compute calibration-in-the-large per group. Healthy: positive net benefit across the clinically relevant threshold range (e.g., 0.05-0.30) for every subgroup. Unhealthy: net benefit drops below treat-all/none for minority groups, meaning the model would harm rather than help triage decisions for those patients.
possible_sources: Vickers & Elkin, 'Decision Curve Analysis: A Novel Method for Evaluating Prediction Models', Med Decis Making 2006
Van Calster et al., 'Reporting and Interpreting Decision Curve Analysis', European Urology 2018
Pfohl et al., 'Net benefit, calibration, threshold selection, and training objectives for algorithmic fairness in healthcare', FAccT 2022
